text: Some time later, Absalom son of David had a beautiful sister named Tamar, and Amnon, David's son, fell in love with her.
Amnon was so tormented over Tamar that he made himself sick, since she was a virgin and it seemed impossible to get near her.
Amnon had a friend named Jonadab, son of David's brother Shimeah, a very shrewd man.
Jonadab asked Amnon why he looked so run-down morning after morning, and Amnon admitted he was in love with Tamar, his brother Absalom's sister.
Jonadab told him to lie down and pretend to be ill, and when his father came to see him, to ask that Tamar be sent to feed him by hand so he could watch her cook.
So Amnon lay down and pretended to be sick; when the king came to see him, Amnon asked that Tamar come and make a couple of cakes in his sight so he could eat from her hand.
David sent word to Tamar at the palace to go to her brother Amnon's house and prepare food for him.
Tamar went to Amnon's house, where he was lying down; she took dough, kneaded it, and made cakes in front of him.
She took the pan and set the food out before him, but he refused to eat and ordered everyone out of the room, and they all left.
Amnon told Tamar to bring the food into his bedroom so he could eat from her hand, and Tamar took the cakes she had made and brought them to him there.
But when she brought them near for him to eat, he grabbed her and said, come lie with me, my sister.
She answered, no, my brother, do not force me — such a thing should not be done in Israel; do not do this outrageous thing.
She said she would carry the shame forever, and he would become like one of the fools in Israel; she urged him instead to speak to the king, who would not refuse to give her to him.
But he would not listen to her, and since he was stronger than she was, he forced her and raped her.
Then Amnon hated her with a hatred far greater than the love he had felt, and he told her to get up and leave.
She protested that sending her away now would be an even greater wrong than what he had already done, but he refused to listen.
He called his young servant and told him to put this woman out and bolt the door behind her.
She was wearing an ornamented robe, the kind worn by the king's virgin daughters; the servant put her out and bolted the door after her.
Tamar put ashes on her head, tore the ornamented robe she was wearing, put her hand on her head, and went away crying loudly.

Her brother Absalom asked if Amnon had been with her, and told her to keep quiet for now since he was her brother, and not to take it to heart; so Tamar lived in her brother Absalom's house, a desolate woman.
When King David heard all this, he was furious.
Absalom never said a word to Amnon, good or bad, but he hated Amnon for violating his sister Tamar.
Two full years later, Absalom held a sheep-shearing feast at Baal-hazor near Ephraim, and he invited all the king's sons.
Absalom went to the king and asked him to come with his servants to the shearing feast.
The king said he would not come himself, so as not to be a burden, but he blessed Absalom when pressed.
Absalom then asked that, if not the king, at least his brother Amnon go with them; the king asked why Amnon should go.
But Absalom kept pressing until David let Amnon and all his other sons go with him.

Absalom ordered his servants to watch until Amnon's heart was merry with wine, and then, at his signal, to strike Amnon down and kill him — telling them not to be afraid, since he himself was giving the command; they should be bold and brave.
Absalom's servants did to Amnon just as Absalom had ordered; all the king's other sons got up, mounted their mules, and fled.
While they were still on the road, a report reached David that Absalom had struck down all the king's sons, leaving not one of them alive.
The king got up, tore his clothes, and lay on the ground, and all his servants standing nearby tore their clothes too.

But Jonadab, son of David's brother Shimeah, spoke up and said the king should not think all the young men, the king's sons, had been killed — only Amnon was dead, since Absalom had planned this from the day Amnon violated his sister Tamar.
So the king should not take the report to heart as though all his sons were dead — only Amnon was dead.
Meanwhile Absalom fled. The young watchman looked up and saw a large group coming down the road from the hillside.
Jonadab told the king, look, the king's sons are coming, just as your servant said.
As soon as he finished speaking, the king's sons arrived and wept loudly, and the king and all his servants wept bitterly as well.
But Absalom fled and went to Talmai son of Ammihud, king of Geshur; David mourned for his son every day.
Absalom stayed in Geshur three years after he fled there.
And King David's heart longed to go out to Absalom, since he had been consoled over Amnon's death.
